description: Relative Tightness Index and Equity Yield Slope.Panel A shows the time series of the RT index (standardized). Panel B displays the realized and fitted term structure slope of forward equity yields. The realized slope is measured as the difference between 5-year and 1-year forward equity yields. The fitted slope values are obtained by estimating a linear regression using the equation ɛe5,tf−e1,tf=ϕ0+ϕ1RTt+ɛt.Here, RTt refers to the relative tightness index, and e5,tf and e1,tf represent forward equity yields from S&P 500 index dividend futures contracts. Additionally, we include the estimated equity yield slope from Giglio et al. (2023) as an out-of-sample test. The shaded areas in the figures indicate NBER recessions.

Asset Prices in Intermediary Model.This figure illustrates the asset prices and properties of the intermediary stochastic discount factor (SDF) as functions of the state variable: bank net worth share nˆ. Panel A shows the marginal value of bank net worth, Panel B shows the volatility of the intermediary SDF, Panel C shows the conditional expected returns of risky assets, and Panel D shows the conditional volatility of risky assets. The term nˆss denotes the steady-state net worth share in this economy, suggested by a long simulation from the model, nˆb denotes the binding threshold, and nˆ≤nˆb is the region in which the constraint is binding. The parameters are based on the benchmark calibration summarized in Table 5.

Equity Yields in Intermediary Model.Panel A shows the one-period equity yield as a function of state variable: bank net worth share, nˆ. The arrows show the direction in which the state variable is expected to move. Panel B shows the decomposition of the term structure slope of equity yields from Eq. (7). The term slope is decomposed into the equity term premium and expected changes in yields, plotted as functions of nˆ. The term nˆss denotes the steady-state net worth share in this economy, suggested by a long simulation from the model, nˆb denotes the binding threshold, and nˆ≤nˆb is the region in which the constraint is binding. The parameters are based on the benchmark calibration summarized in Table 5.

Equity Yield Slope during Financial Crisis: Data versus Model.This figure illustrates the equity yield slopes during the 2008 financial crisis. The realized slope in the data is measured as the difference between 5-year and 1-year forward equity yields based on S&P 500 dividend futures contracts. The model-implied yield slope is computed by feeding real shocks to the marginal value of net worth into the intermediary model. Specifically, we begin by presuming that the economy in the first quarter of 2007 was at a steady state. We then use shocks to the RT index as a basis to determine equivalent shocks to the net worth’s marginal value in the model. Concretely, this relationship is represented as: log(μt+1)−log(μt)=ΔRTt+1/σΔRT×σΔlog(μ),where σΔRT represents the sample standard deviation of RT index shocks, and σΔlog(μ) denote the standard deviation of log(μt) shocks from a long simulation in the model.

Convenience Yields in Intermediary Model.Panel A shows the convenience yield and expected 2-year government bond excess return as functions of state variable: bank net worth share, nˆ. Panel B shows the decomposition of the term structure slope of convenience yields. The slope is decomposed into the term premium of convenience yields and expected changes in convenience yields, plotted as functions of nˆ. The term nˆss denotes the steady-state net worth share in this economy, suggested by a long simulation from the model, nˆb denotes the binding threshold, and nˆ≤nˆb is the region in which the constraint is binding. The parameters are based on the benchmark calibration summarized in Table 5.

Convenience Yields over Time.The figure is divided into four panels. The Panels A and B display the monthly time series of convenience yields in both U.S. and Euro markets. The Panels C and D provide a detailed look at the daily series during two significant periods: the financial crisis and the COVID-19 crisis. The convenience yield is calculated as the difference between the box rate and the corresponding Treasury yield with the same maturity, following the methodology in Van Binsbergen et al. (2021).The data source is Diamond and Van Tassel (2021).

Discount Rate Slope vs. Dividend Growth Slope in the Extended Model.Panel A illustrates the expected one-period growth rate as a function of the state variable: bank net worth share, nˆ. The arrows show the direction in which the state variable is expected to move. Panel B shows the decomposition of the equity yield slope into the discount rate slope and the dividend growth slope, each as a function of nˆ. The term nˆss denotes the steady-state net worth share in this economy, suggested by a long simulation from the model. nˆb denotes the binding threshold, and nˆ≤nˆb is the region in which the constraint is binding. The parameters are detailed in Section 6.2.
